era_context: The early 12th century: the Crusader States are consolidating in the Levant following the First Crusade; the Song Dynasty rules southern China while the Jin Dynasty (Jurchen) is rising in the north; the Norman kingdoms are expanding in England and Sicily; and Western Christendom is experiencing the final stages of the Investiture Controversy.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Central Asia and the Steppe (limited non-Islamic sources)
Mesoamerica and South America (no written sources)

events.title: Crusader siege and capture of Caesarea
events.description: The Crusaders, under the leadership of King Baldwin I of Jerusalem and other Crusader lords, besieged and captured the port city of Caesarea (Qaisariyya) in May 1117. The city had been an important Muslim stronghold on the Palestinian coast, and its capture was a significant strategic victory for the Crusaders. The siege lasted several months, and the Crusaders employed siege engines and naval support to overcome the city's defenses. The capture of Caesarea strengthened Crusader control over the Palestinian coast and provided them with an important port facility.
key_figures: King Baldwin I of Jerusalem (Crusader leader)
events.certainty_note: The capture of Caesarea in 1117 is well-attested in multiple Crusader chronicles. The date is securely established.

events.title: Death of King Baldwin I of Jerusalem
events.description: King Baldwin I of Jerusalem died on 2 April 1118 (note: this event is dated to 1118, not 1117; see correction below). However, in 1117, Baldwin I was still actively ruling the Kingdom of Jerusalem and leading military campaigns against Muslim forces. His reign (1100–1118) had been marked by the consolidation of Crusader power in the Levant and the expansion of Crusader territory. Baldwin I was succeeded by his cousin Baldwin II, who would continue the process of Crusader state-building.
events.certainty_note: Baldwin I's death is dated to 2 April 1118 in most sources, not 1117. However, his reign and activities in 1117 are well-documented.

events.title: Continued Song-Jin military conflict in northern China
events.description: The Song Dynasty, under Emperor Huizong, continued to face military pressure from the Jin Dynasty (Jurchen) in northern China. The Jin had recently established control over Manchuria and were beginning to encroach on Song territory. These military conflicts were part of the broader process of the Jin Dynasty's rise to power in East Asia. The Song Dynasty would eventually lose significant northern territories to the Jin in the coming decades, leading to the establishment of the Jin Dynasty as a major power in China.

sources.name: Zizhi Tongjian (Comprehensive Mirror for Aid in Government)
Song Shi (History of the Song Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Song-Jin military conflict is well-established, but specific campaigns in 1117 are less precisely dated in available sources.

events.title: Anund Jacob of Sweden continues reign; consolidation of royal authority
events.description: King Anund Jacob of Sweden (r. 1069–1119 or 1120) was in the later years of his reign in 1117. His reign was marked by efforts to strengthen royal authority and promote Christianity throughout Sweden. Anund Jacob was involved in conflicts with his brother Inge the Younger over succession and royal power. Swedish sources for this period are limited, and much of what is known comes from later compilations and Scandinavian chronicles.

sources.name: Swedish royal chronicles and later compilations
Saxo Grammaticus, Gesta Danorum (History of the Danes)

events.certainty_note: Swedish sources for the early 12th century are sparse and often compiled centuries later. The specific events of 1117 are not precisely documented.

events.title: Norman consolidation in Sicily and southern Italy
events.region: Kingdom of Sicily and southern Italy
events.description: The Norman kingdom in Sicily and southern Italy continued to consolidate its power in 1117. The kingdom was ruled by a regency for the young Roger II, who would eventually become one of the most powerful rulers in the Mediterranean. The Normans were actively engaged in establishing their authority over the diverse populations of Sicily and southern Italy, including Greeks, Arabs, and Italians. The kingdom would become a major power in the Mediterranean over the following decades.

sources.name: Falcandus, Liber de Regno Sicilie (Book of the Kingdom of Sicily)

events.certainty_note: The broader context of Norman consolidation in Sicily is well-established. The specific events of 1117 are less precisely documented.

disconfirming_evidence: The capture of Caesarea in 1117 is sometimes portrayed as a major turning point in Crusader fortunes, but modern scholarship emphasizes that the Crusaders faced continued resistance from Muslim forces and that the consolidation of Crusader power was a gradual process extending over several decades. The capture of Caesarea was significant but was one of many military victories during this period.
historiographic_note: The year 1117 is moderately well-documented in Western European sources, particularly regarding Crusader military activities in the Levant. Chinese sources (Song and Jin dynasties) are abundant but often focused on court politics rather than military campaigns. Islamic sources provide parallel accounts of Crusader activities in the Levant. Northern European sources are sparse and often compiled centuries after the events. Sub-Saharan Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia have virtually no written sources for this year.

graph_edges.note: The capture of Caesarea occurred during Baldwin I's reign, which ended in 1118.
Baldwin I's death in 1118 led to the succession of Baldwin II.

_meta.note: Produced via Claude Code CLI (Max subscription) before API migration
